data.title: Biggest stock movers Tuesday: ERIC, BA, C, HPE, and more
data.description:
Stock futures ticked higher on Tuesday as investors monitored the next moves in President Donald Trump’s evolving tariff agenda. The White House has intensified scrutiny of pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports, hinting at possible tariffs targeting those sectors.

Here are some of Tuesday's biggest stock movers:

Biggest stock gainers

  • Ericsson (NASDAQ:ERIC) +6% – Shares rose after the company reported Q1 results with GAAP EPS of SEK 1.24 and revenue of SEK 55.03B, up 3.2% Y/Y. The adjusted gross margin improved to 48.5% from 42.7%, while EBITA came in at SEK 6.9B (12.6% margin) and free cash flow before M&A stood at SEK 2.7B. Ericsson expressed confidence in its Mobile Networks business and expects the Enterprise segment to stabilize in 2025. The company approved a 2024 dividend of SEK 2.85 per share, to be paid in two installments. Looking ahead, Ericsson flagged ongoing macro and trade uncertainties, projecting Q2 adjusted gross margin in the 48–50% range, factoring in a roughly 1-point hit from tariffs, while sales across Networks and Cloud segments are expected to align with seasonal norms.
  • Citigroup (C) +4% – Shares gained after the bank topped Q1 earnings and revenue expectations, driven by solid gains in its markets and investment banking units. Despite the beat, Citi slightly trimmed its FY2025 revenue outlook to $83.1B–$84.1B, down from a prior range of $83.5B–$84.5B, while maintaining guidance for net interest income growth of 2%–3% (excluding markets) and projecting annual expenses to land just below $53.4B. CEO Jane Fraser highlighted "continued momentum, positive operating leverage, and improved returns" across all five business segments.
  • Bank of America (BAC) +5% – Shares rallied after the bank posted better-than-expected earnings and revenue, fueled by strength in its Global Markets unit. GAAP EPS rose 18% Y/Y, supported by higher net interest and fee income, along with the division’s 12th straight quarter of sales and trading revenue growth, according to CEO Brian Moynihan. Looking ahead, BofA projects Q4 net interest income of $15.5B–$15.7B on a fully taxable-equivalent basis, compared to $14.6B in Q1 and in line with the $15.6B consensus.
  • Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) +7% – Shares surged on reports that Elliott Investment Management has built a stake exceeding $1.5B in the company, making it one of HPE’s top five shareholders, according to Bloomberg data. The activist investor is expected to engage with the software and networking firm to explore ways to enhance shareholder value, though its specific intentions have not yet been revealed.

Biggest stock losers

  • Applied Digital (NASDAQ:APLD) -11% – Shares sank after the company delivered mixed FQ3 results, with revenue missing expectations despite a 22% Y/Y increase and narrower losses. The company also announced plans to divest its Cloud Services Business, which may shift to an on-demand GPU cluster model, opening new revenue opportunities. Management reaffirmed the importance of the Ellendale campus, with its first building slated to go live by the end of 2025. Leasing updates are expected soon, and the company sees continued demand from hyperscalers along with strong growth in HPC data center capacity.
  • Boeing (NYSE:BA) -3% – Shares fell after reports that China directed its airlines to suspend deliveries of Boeing jets amid escalating U.S.-China trade tensions. The move follows President Trump's new tariffs of up to 145% on Chinese goods, prompting Beijing’s retaliatory 125% duties on U.S. imports. With China projected to drive 20% of global aircraft demand over the next two decades, the halt adds pressure to Boeing, which has already faced challenges securing major Chinese orders. The tariffs could effectively double the cost of U.S.-made aircraft, making further deliveries economically unfeasible. China is also exploring support measures for airlines leasing Boeing jets affected by the dispute.
  • Allegro MicroSystems (ALGM) -9% – Shares slid after ON Semiconductor (ON) withdrew its $6.9B takeover bid, citing Allegro’s board’s reluctance to fully engage with the offer. Onsemi had proposed $35.10 per share, which Allegro rejected last month as undervaluing the company. Onsemi noted it had attempted to initiate discussions over the past six months, but ultimately decided to pull the proposal. Instead, the company will focus on returning capital to shareholders through its existing buyback program.
  • Albertsons (ACI) -7% – Shares dropped after the grocer issued soft FY2025 EPS guidance, overshadowing a stronger-than-expected Q4 profit. The company expects adjusted EPS between $2.03 and $2.16, below the $2.30 consensus, and forecasts identical sales growth of 1.5%–2.5% with adjusted EBITDA of $3.8B–$3.9B, down from $4B last year. Labeling FY2025 as an “investment year,” Albertsons anticipates stronger performance in FY2026, targeting over 2% identical sales growth and faster adjusted EBITDA gains.

data.title: Wells Fargo’s Animal Spirits Index plunges below zero, signaling economic pessimism amid tariff uncertainty
data.description:
Wells Fargo Investment Institute’s Animal Spirits Index fell to -0.34 in March, marking its first dip into negative territory since October 2023, according to the investment bank.

Analysts attributed the decline to mounting tariff uncertainty and weakening consumer sentiment, signaling growing pessimism about the near-term economic outlook.

“Values above zero reflect optimism, while negative readings indicate pessimism,” Azhar Iqbal, director and econometrician for Wells Fargo's Corporate and Investment Bank, said in a note.

 

 

The index tracks five variables: the S&P 500 (SP500), Consumer Confidence Index, yield curve spread, VIX volatility index (VIX), and Economic Policy Uncertainty Index, created by Baker, Bloom and Davis to measure movements in policy related to economic uncertainty.

According to Wells Fargo, rising policy uncertainty and market volatility inversely impact the index. Most components dragged the ASI lower in March, with the S&P 500 (SP500) plunging 343 basis points — its steepest monthly drop since 2022 — and consumer confidence hitting a four-year low of 92.9.

In addition, the Economic Policy Uncertainty Index surged to 297, its highest post-COVID level, which Wells Fargo analysts called “a direct reflection of trade policy whiplash.” Only the yield curve’s persistent positivity provided modest relief, the investment bank noted. The index’s decline echoes patterns seen during the Great Recession, according to Wells Fargo analysts, who have tracked the metric’s behavior since 1967.

Iqbal also said the current environment shares similarities with past cycles, marked by sluggish recoveries and prolonged policy headwinds.

Looking ahead, the investment bank warned of a “modest stagflationary shock” from tariffs, forecasting negative real GDP growth and 125 basis points of Fed rate cuts by late 2025.

“Elevated uncertainty will likely keep the ASI in the red for months,” analysts concluded, citing stalled trade negotiations and inflationary pressures.

data.title: Galapagos' CFO announces departure
data.description:
• Galapagos NV (NASDAQ:GLPG) said on Tuesday that its CFO and COO, Thad Huston, will depart effective as of August 1, 2025.
  • Huston has decided to leave the company and return to the U.S. for personal and professional reasons, the company said.

data.title: LVMH Q1 miss casts shadow over luxury sector
data.description:
LVMH Moët Hennessy’s (LVMHF, LVMUY) shares were under heavy selling pressure on the Paris stock exchange as unexpectedly weak first quarter results sent shockwaves through the luxury sector, driving shares of rival brands Kering SA (OTCPK:PPRUY), Hermes International (HESAY, HESAF), and Prada (PRDSF, PRDSY) all lower in sympathy.

The drop in shares also briefly allowed Hermes (HESAY, HESAF) to overtake LVMH (LVMHF, LVMUY) in market capitalization, causing the Paris-based conglomerate to momentarily lose its top spot as the world’s number one luxury brand.

Investors on both sides of the Atlantic were rattled by not only a contraction in sales – specifically in LVMH’s (LVMHF, LVMUY) centerpiece Louis Vuitton and Dior brands – but by the “magnitude of the deterioration in the company’s largest division,” Goldman Sach’s Louise Singlehurst noted.

And given that the Q1 miss was attributed to slowdown in spending in China and Japan, “this means we haven’t yet seen the impact of recent events upon the business,” said Singlehurst’s colleague Natasha de la Grense.

Despite CFO Cecile Cabanis’ efforts to put a positive spin on the company’s businesses during the Q1 earnings call, she eventually conceded that LVMH (LVMHF, LVMUY) faces “macro uncertainties and lack of visibility on external factors,” but would not offer any projections on the possible impact from tariffs and the knock-down effect on consumer demand.

Analysts, however, were less forgiving and lowered estimates to address slower demand. Morgan Stanley models another contraction in sales in the second quarter, and if correct, would be the first time in more than 30 years that the Fashion & Leather Goods division posts four quarters in a row of sales contraction.

“The major difference this time (from two quarters of contraction in 2020) is that demand from China is a major drag and the industry is now facing a softer macro environment worldwide after having raised prices significantly,” said Morgan analyst James Grzinic.

While CFO Cabanis was noticeably vague about tariffs, China, and the U.S. consumer, she conceded that the company will remain “alert and vigilant” and disciplined in its resource allocation.

“Given the uncertainty that we’ve been very mindful and very disciplined on the pace at which we allocate resources, that it’s more difficult to control COGS than OpEX,” Cabanis said on the call.

At the U.S. open, shares of LVMH Moet Hennessy (LVMHF, LVMUY) are down 6% and in the red for a second day.

data.title: Canada inflation records at eight-month low of 2.3%
data.description:
• The annual inflation rate in Canada fell to 2.3% in March from eight-month high of 2.6% in the previous month, below market expectations that it would remain at 2.6%.
  • The figure also missed the central bank forecast of 2.5%.
  • The decrease kicked off the normalization of the inflation rate forecasted by the BoC this year after end of goods and services tax and harmonized tax breaks halfway last month triggered a 0.6 percentage point increase in the headline inflation rate.
  • Prices reversed for gasoline amid the aggressive drop in crude oil prices after OPEC+ confirmed plans to raise output, driving transportation inflation to slow.

  • The Consumer Price Index increased 0.30% in March over the previous month.

  • ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWC), (NYSEARCA:FLCA).
  • Currency: (USD:CAD) (USD:CAD)

data.title: Molson Coors could shift strategy with a new CEO on the way
data.description:
Molson Coors Beverage Company (NYSE:TAP) opened slightly lower on Tuesday after announcing that CEO Gavin Hattersley, will retire from the company and the board before December 31, 2025. Notably, Hattersley has been at the company for 28 years and was instrumental in the creation of the MillerCoors joint venture between Molson Coors and SABMiller. He also served as CEO of Molson Coors (NYSE:TAP) for the last six years.

TD Cowen analyst Robert Moskow thinks that Molson Coors (TAP) describing the search as including both internal and external without a named successor in place could indicate that the beer giant will prioritize external candidates. Interestingly, Moskow reasoned that could mean that the board is open to new ideas for creating shareholder value in the future.

"What we would like to know (but don't expect to learn) is whether the company will seek a change agent for the CEO role or just someone to continue executing the current strategy," updated Moskow. He said the beer company's string of pearls approach has entailed deploying cash flow toward acquisitions of high-growth emerging brands and licenses, while also consolidating breweries to adjust to structural declines in mainstream beer. However, while that strategy has delivered strong EPS growth, Moskow said the stock's low valuation multiple reflects the market's lack of confidence in TAP's organic growth profile.

data.title: Mali authorities shut Barrick Gold's Bamako office over alleged non-payment of taxes - Reuters
data.description:
Government authorities in Mali have shut Barrick Gold's (NYSE:GOLD) office in the capital Bamako over the alleged non-payment of taxes, Reuters reported Tuesday, in the latest escalation of a long-standing dispute over mining revenues.

Staff in Bamako cannot access the office premises, although the closure does not affect Barrick's (NYSE:GOLD) Loulo-Gounkoto mining complex, where operations have been suspended since mid-January.

The miner and the Mali government have been mired in a dispute since 2023 over the implementation of a new mining code that gives the government a greater share in Loulo-Gounkoto.

The two sides have been negotiating to resolve the dispute, and Reuters reported on February 19 that Barrick (GOLD) had signed an agreement that still awaits approval from the government; Mali was asking the company for a 125B CFA francs (~$216M) settlement payment, and for agreement to the new mining code that came into force in 2023.

data.title: Software stocks show greater resilience under tariff, macro uncertainties: Mizuho
data.description:
Software stocks with high levels of recurring revenue demonstrate greater resilience to the effects of tariffs and economic concerns, and Mizuho believes many will report better-than-expected financial results in the upcoming earnings season.

"While most software companies are not directly affected by tariff increases, they remain vulnerable to macro uncertainty and potential recessionary pressures," said Mizuho analysts, led by Siti Panigrahi, in an investor note. "We estimate that a one-percentage-point change in GDP growth could lead to a 1–3 percentage point shift in software revenue growth. This relationship is reasonable given the high proportion of recurring revenue (~91% across our coverage), which offers greater resilience compared to businesses reliant on unit/license or pricing swings."

"While we see no direct impact from tariffs on our covered companies (except few with consumer exposure) and expect strong March quarter results, we see early signs of delay in deal closings due to uncertain macro backdrop," Panigrahi added. "Furthermore, we believe, most companies have provided conservative initial FY guidance, offering enough cushion to maintain outlooks despite macro uncertainty, but could offer cautious commentary on Q1 calls."

These factors, combined with the recent market selloff, prompted Mizuho to increase price targets across an array of software stocks:

  • Autodesk (NASDAQ:ADSK) increased to $400 from $350
  • Cadence Design Systems (NASDAQ:CDNS) to $350 from $300
  • Five9 (NASDAQ:FIVN) to $55 from $42
  • HubSpot (NYSE:HUBS) to $900 from $700
  • Intuit (INTU) to $765 from $700
  • MongoDB (MDB) to $250 from $190
  • Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) to $210 from $180
  • Procore (NYSE:PCOR) to $75 from $65
  • Synopsys (SNPS) to $650 from $600
  • Twilio (NYSE:TWLO) to $165 from $125
  • Workday (NASDAQ:WDAY) to $320 from $275
  • Zoom (NASDAQ:ZM) to $105 from $95

Valuation multiples have also declined significantly year to date.

"We observe the multiples have compressed significantly during the year so far with a decline of ~21% since the start of the year from 6.8x to 5.4x," Panigrahi noted. "Particularly, the current 5.4x average NTM EV/Revenue multiple across the software space is roughly ~13% discount to a rolling 2-year average of 6.2x, implying an attractive entry point if the economy does not tip into a major recession."

data.title: CleanSpark increases Coinbase Prime credit facility, launches Bitcoin treasury desk
data.description:
Bitcoin miner CleanSpark (NASDAQ:CLSK) announced on Tuesday an increase to its Coinbase Prime credit facility to $200M and the launch of an institutional-grade Bitcoin treasury desk.

"We are proud to expand our relationship with Coinbase (COIN) through their Bitcoin-collateralized lending program as part of our broader strategic approach to capital management," said CleanSpark CEO Zach Bradford.

"With our Bitcoin holdings now exceeding 12,000, valued at approximately $1 billion, we believe this is the right time to evolve from a nearly 100% hold strategy adopted in mid-2023 and move back using a portion of our monthly production to support operations," said Bradford.
